title: Due process and judicial compensation

audience_match: Foreign investors in Philippine public utilities (audience by interest)
Members of the Supreme Court (diplomatic critique addressed to colleagues)
Members of Congress (for the funding recommendation)
Justices and judges of the trial and appellate courts (the SAJ beneficiaries)
Inquirer Opinion readers and constitutional-law practitioners
Department of Budget and Management officials

keywords: Special Allowance for Justices and Judges
primary_topics: Critique of Gamboa vs Teves (June 28, 2011) for procedural-due-process failure: foreign investors who relied on decades-old SEC interpretation were never summoned or heard before being forced to unload investments
Restatement of the Themistocles/Webster due-process tradition: 'Strike, but hear me first' / 'a law that hears before it condemns'
Separate column-topic: explanation of the Supreme Court's June 7, 2011 resolution implementing the long-delayed judicial compensation adjustment
How RA 9227 (effective November 11, 2006) doubled magistrate pay via the Special Allowance for Justices and Judges (SAJ) — fully implemented during CJP's term
The new resolution's tax-free SAJ structure, retroactive to June 24, 2010, and its problems (rank-and-file employees losing SAJ surpluses; possible docket-fee increases on poor litigants)
sub_topics: Gamboa vs Teves decision date: June 28, 2011; ponente Justice Antonio T. Carpio
SEC's uniform interpretation since 1935 — 'capital' includes both voting and non-voting shares
Foreigners 'enticed' by SEC interpretation; their investments 'forced to unload at bargain prices'
Manuel V. Pangilinan sued 'in his capacity as managing director of First Pacific Co. Ltd.' but First Pacific itself not impleaded
Foreign chambers of commerce also not impleaded
Bill of Rights Section 1: 'No person shall be deprived of life, liberty or property without due process of law' — 'person' includes foreigners and corporations
RA 9227 — SAJ funded by Supreme Court-authorized docket-fee increases
600 trial-court vacancies (about 30%) prior to the pay increase
Two 10% basic-pay increases under President Gloria Macapagal-Arroyo — charged to SAJ, subjected to withholding tax ? judicial take-home pay effectively DECREASED
Joint Resolution No. 4 of June 17, 2009 — Salary Standardization Law revision with four yearly tranches; magistrates' tranches again charged to SAJ
New SC resolution dated June 7, 2011 — base pay sourced from national government; increases sourced from SAJ; DBM covers deficiency; retroactive to June 24, 2010, tax-free
Problems: SAJ insufficient ? rank-and-file lose surpluses ? possible docket-fee increases burden poor litigants
CJP's recommendation: Congress should fund pay increases from national coffers (like other government offices)

signature_phrases.phrase: a law that hears before it condemns, proceeds upon inquiry and renders judgment only after trial
no one, not even the highest court of the land
the absolute need for due process without which any decision would be void
I will refrain from discussing the substance of the ponencia
instead of being increased, judicial take home pay was effectively decreased!
to the chagrin of poor litigants

people: Artemio V. Panganiban (columnist; former CJ who fully implemented SAJ)
Manuel V. Pangilinan (named defendant in Gamboa)
cases: Gamboa vs Teves (June 28, 2011) — 60-40 capital limited to voting shares only; ponente Carpio
laws_treaties: 1987 Philippine Constitution — Bill of Rights Section 1 (due process)
1987 Philippine Constitution — Article XII Section 11 (60% Filipino capital for public utilities)
Republic Act 9227 — doubled magistrate compensation via SAJ (effective November 11, 2006)
Memorandum of Agreement between DBM and Philippine Judges Association (April 6, 2011)
Supreme Court Resolution (June 7, 2011) — implementing SAJ + base-pay structure

stances.claim: Gamboa vs Teves (June 28, 2011) is procedurally void for due-process failure: the foreign investors who, since 1935, relied on the SEC's uniform interpretation of 'capital' as including both voting and non-voting shares — and who would now be forced to unload their investments at bargain prices — were never summoned, served, or given the opportunity to defend themselves. The Bill of Rights' word 'person' clearly includes foreigners and corporations.
stances.rhetorical_move: procedural-due-process objection that brackets the merits ('one may even agree with the substantive restriction, but...')
stances.confidence: asserted with 'with due respect' — diplomatic critique of colleagues' ruling

stances.claim: Due process is the absolute prerequisite for any valid decision — 'without which any decision would be void' — traceable to Themistocles ('Strike, but hear me first!') and Daniel Webster's classic definition ('a law that hears before it condemns'). No one, not even the highest court of the land, may impose substantive restrictions without first hearing those most adversely affected.
stances.rhetorical_move: natural-law-tradition citation + 'not even the highest court' as judicial-humility check

stances.claim: The new judicial compensation structure (June 7, 2011 SC Resolution) stabilizes judges' take-home pay by routing the SAJ tax-free, but creates two new problems: rank-and-file court employees lose SAJ surpluses, and the SC may be forced to raise docket fees, burdening poor litigants. The proper solution is for Congress to fund judicial pay increases from national coffers — as it does for other government offices.

notable_anecdotes.summary: Personal disclosure of institutional history: RA 9227's doubled magistrate compensation via SAJ was 'fully implemented during my tenure as chief justice' — the pay increase was necessary to fill 600 trial-court vacancies (about 30% of the bench) which were causing delays.
Anti-graft rationale stated openly: 'decent compensation, I thought, would discourage graft in the judiciary.'
Counter-intuitive math: under President Arroyo's two 10% pay increases charged to SAJ and made taxable, 'instead of being increased, judicial take home pay was effectively decreased!'

Manuel V. Pangilinan was sued as managing director of First Pacific — but First Pacific itself was not impleaded, nor were the foreign chambers of commerce — a procedural gap CJP flags as constitutional violation.

decision_framework_signals: applies procedural due process as threshold inquiry — even concurring substance doesn't cure procedural void
uses Themistocles-Webster natural-law tradition as the foundational due-process anchor
treats 'person' in Bill of Rights broadly to include foreigners and corporations
applies relied-upon-government-representation principle (SEC's pre-1935-onward interpretation) as creating procedural standing
frames judicial-compensation policy in terms of: anti-vacancy + anti-graft + non-burden-on-poor-litigants
treats Congress-funded compensation (rather than docket-fee-funded SAJ) as the equity-preserving structural solution

register_markers: exclamation-point emphasis on counterintuitive consequence ('effectively decreased!')
matter-of-fact technical explanation register for compensation mechanics
subheadings within the column ('Foreign investors not heard', 'Adjusted judicial compensation', 'More problems')
one_paragraph_summary: Published July 3, 2011 in two paired parts. The first critiques Gamboa vs Teves (June 28, 2011, penned by Justice Antonio T. Carpio) — which limited the 60-40 Filipino-capital rule to voting common shares only — on procedural-due-process grounds. Since 1935, the SEC has uniformly held that 'capital' includes both voting and non-voting shares; foreign investors relied on this and invested accordingly. Yet none of them were summoned, served, or heard before the SC ruling that would force them to unload their investments at bargain prices. Manuel V. Pangilinan was sued as managing director of First Pacific, but First Pacific itself was not impleaded, nor were the foreign chambers of commerce. CJP invokes Themistocles ('Strike, but hear me first!') and Daniel Webster ('a law that hears before it condemns, proceeds upon inquiry and renders judgment only after trial') and concludes 'with due respect' that 'no one, not even the highest court of the land' may impose such a restriction without hearing those most adversely affected — bracketing the merits to focus on procedure. The second part explains the Supreme Court's June 7, 2011 resolution implementing the long-delayed Memorandum of Agreement (April 6, 2011) between the DBM and the Philippine Judges Association. RA 9227 (effective November 11, 2006), fully implemented during CJP's tenure as Chief Justice, doubled magistrate compensation via the SAJ — necessary to fill 600 trial-court vacancies (~30%) and to discourage graft. But two subsequent rounds of pay increases (Arroyo's twin 10% increments and the 2009 Joint Resolution No. 4 tranches) were charged to the SAJ and made taxable, so 'instead of being increased, judicial take home pay was effectively decreased!' The new SC resolution restores tax-free treatment retroactive to June 24, 2010, but creates new problems: SAJ surpluses for rank-and-file vanish, and possible docket-fee increases would burden poor litigants. CJP's recommended fix: Congress should fund magistrate pay increases from national coffers, as it does for other government offices.
